bibliographic_data.title: Crowd-Assisted Polyp Annotation of Virtual Colonoscopy Videos
bibliographic_data.abstract: Virtual colonoscopy (VC) allows a radiologist to navigate through a 3D colon model reconstructed from a computed tomography scan of the abdomen, looking for polyps. Polyps are seen as protrusions on the colon wall and haustral folds, visible in VC fly-through videos. A complete review of the colon surface requires full navigation from the rectum to the cecum in antegrade and retrograde directions, which is a tedious task that takes an average of 30 minutes. Crowdsourcing is used for the examination of complete VC fly-through videos for polyp annotation by non-experts. The crowdsourcing interface includes an interactive tool for the crowd to annotate suspected polyps in the video with an enclosing box. Using our workflow, we achieve an overall polyps-per-patient sensitivity of 87.88% (95.65% for polyps ≥5mm and 70% for polyps <5mm). We also demonstrate the efficacy and effectiveness of a non-expert user in detecting and annotating polyps and discuss their possibility in aiding radiologists in VC examinations.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents the use of crowdsourcing for the annotation of polyps in virtual colonoscopy videos, achieving a high sensitivity rate and demonstrating the potential of non-expert users.
core_content.key_methodology: Crowdsourcing is employed using an interactive tool where non-experts annotate suspected polyps with enclosing boxes. The study uses complete fly-through videos from the rectum to cecum in both supine and prone orientations, generated by a commercially available virtual colonoscopy system.
outcomes: Achieved an overall polyps-per-patient sensitivity of 87.88% (95.65% for polyps ≥5mm and 70% for polyps <5mm)
Demonstrated the efficacy and effectiveness of non-expert users in detecting and annotating polyps
Discussed the potential of crowdsourcing to aid radiologists in VC examinations
core_content.full_text_markdown: Virtual colonoscopy (VC) is a technique that uses CT scanning and advanced visualization techniques to virtually navigate within a reconstructed 3D colon model, searching for colorectal polyps. One major issue with VC is the time-consuming process of interpreting the results by radiologists. To address this, crowdsourcing is used to engage non-expert users in annotating polyps from complete fly-through videos. The study uses an interactive tool where workers annotate suspected polyps with enclosing boxes. The results show a high sensitivity rate (87.88%) and demonstrate that non-experts can effectively detect and annotate polyps, potentially aiding radiologists in their work.
